answers.a: We can navigate through the linked list in reverse order
answers.b: There is no beginning and no end
answers.c: Searching is faster than in a singly Linked list

explanations.a: Incorrect. Unless it is a doubly circular linked list, reverse traversal is not possible in a standard circular singly linked list.
explanations.b: Correct. In a circular linked list, the last node points back to the first node, so there is no true beginning or end.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Searching is not inherently faster in a circular linked list compared to a singly linked list; both require traversal.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Option b is a valid property of circular linked lists.

questions.question: 2. In a circular linked list, if we insert an element how many pointers do we have to update?

explanations.a: Incorrect. At least two pointers must be updated to maintain the circular structure.
explanations.b: Incorrect. Only updating one pointer would break the circular nature of the list.
explanations.c: Correct. You must update the previous last node to point to the new node, and the new node to point to the first node.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Three pointers do not need to be updated for a single insertion.

questions.question: 3. What is the time complexity for deleting an element from a circular linked list?

explanations.a: Incorrect. Deletion is only O(1) if you have a direct pointer to the node and its previous node, which is not usually the case.
explanations.b: Incorrect. Linked lists do not support logarithmic time operations for deletion.
explanations.c: Correct. In the worst case, you must traverse the list to find the node and its previous node, which takes O(n) time.
explanations.d: Incorrect. O(n^2) is not the time complexity for deletion in linked lists.
